1.exposition: The great “therefore” gathers up the whole argument of the letter so far. Having proved that a sinner is justified by faith apart from works — the burden of the two preceding chapters — Paul now turns from the doctrine to its fruits, and the first is the sweetest: we have peace with God. From that root the blessings unfold outward: access into the grace in which we stand, and a rejoicing hope of the glory of God (vv.1–2). Then Paul says something stranger still — we glory even in tribulations, because affliction sanctified forges a golden chain of endurance, tested character, and hope, a hope that does not disappoint (vv.3–5). The paragraph moves from what is objective and secured (peace, standing, access) to what is subjective and felt (the love of God already poured into the heart by the Spirit), and the two are inseparable, because both flow from the one Christ through the one Spirit.
1.original_language: The first fruit hangs on a famous textual knife-edge: the manuscripts divide between echomen (“we have peace,” indicative) and echōmen (“let us have peace,” hortatory subjunctive), a single long vowel separating a statement of fact from an exhortation. Prosagōgē (“access,” v.2) is the word for a formal introduction — being ushered into a royal presence. The chain of vv.3–4 turns on hypomonē (“endurance,” a standing firm under weight) and dokimē (“tested, approved character” — the proof left in metal that has passed through the fire), while the love of God is ekkechytai (“poured out,” lavishly, flooding the heart) by the Holy Spirit.
1.historical_context: “Peace with God” carries the freight of the Hebrew shalom — not the mere absence of hostility but positive covenant wholeness, a settled right-standing. Gentile hearers would also catch the resonance of Roman pax: the formal end of enmity and the opening of a treaty relation, something objective and public rather than a mood. Prosagōgē had two backgrounds a first-century reader would feel at once — the court official who grants audience with the emperor, and the regulated approach to the divine presence in the temple. The ladder of suffering, endurance, and proven character in vv.3–4 has real parallels in Stoic moral training and Jewish wisdom; but Paul bends both, for his goal is not a self-hardened virtue but a God-given hope confirmed by the Spirit.
1.christ: Every gift in the paragraph is routed “through our Lord Jesus Christ”: peace through him (v.1), access through him (v.2). The Christ who accomplished justification is the mediator through whom the justified now stand in grace and look toward glory. And the love “shed abroad in our hearts” (v.5) comes by the Spirit of Christ — the living link between what he did outside us on the cross and what is felt within us now. So the whole of the Christian’s standing, from the objective peace to the inward assurance, is held in one pair of pierced hands.

verses.note: “Therefore, having been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the pivot: having established justification in the previous chapters, Paul now states its first and blessed fruit — peace. On the wording, Robertson weighs the division between echomen (“we have,” indicative) and echōmen (“let us have,” subjunctive): most editors prefer the subjunctive on manuscript grounds, yet the indicative suits the doctrinal flow, for Paul is declaring a fact, not urging a duty. puritan Henry adds that this peace is more than the ending of a quarrel — it is friendship and loving-kindness, the guilt that bred the enmity now taken away through Christ the peacemaker.

Through Christ “we have access by faith into this grace wherein we stand, and rejoice in hope of the glory of God.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read prosagōgē as introduction or admission — we have been brought into the settled state of favour and there stand firm. From that secure footing springs a forward-looking joy: reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note that justification yields not only present standing but the glad prospect of sharing God’s own glory, the believer already exulting in what is not yet seen.

“And not only so, but we glory in tribulations also: knowing that tribulation worketh patience.” The paradox is deliberate. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson observe that the believer exults not merely in hope but in the affliction itself, finding cause for joy in suffering. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret give the reason: trial, far from destroying faith, strengthens it, working hypomonē (endurance). The Christian thus learns to read hardship not as the frown of God but as the instrument by which the Father trains his children.

“And patience, experience; and experience, hope.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson explain dokimē (rendered “experience”) as proof or tried character — the approvedness of metal that has passed through the fire. reformed Calvin traces the ascending links: tribulation, patience, proof, hope, each step the fruit of grace, so that affliction ends not in despair but in a deepened hope. What sin means for the believer’s ruin, grace overrules for good.

“And hope maketh not ashamed; because the love of God is shed abroad in our hearts by the Holy Ghost.” grammatical-historical Robertson notes this hope will not disappoint or put to shame as worldly hopes do — it is sure. Its ground is “the love of God,” which Robertson and reformed Calvin take (with most) to mean God’s love toward us, ekkechytai — poured out, flooding the heart. eastern Cyril names the agent: the Holy Spirit given to us is the inward witness who sheds that love abroad, sealing the assurance of a hope that cannot fail.

witnesses.point: Justification, established in the previous chapters, yields its first fruit here — peace with God — followed by access, standing in grace, and the hope of glory.
Tribulation strengthens rather than destroys faith; the chain of endurance, proof, and hope is grace’s own work, so the believer glories even in affliction.
Peace with God is more than the end of enmity — it is friendship and loving-kindness, the guilt removed through Christ the peacemaker.
The Holy Spirit given to believers is the inward witness who pours out the sense of God’s love, grounding a hope that does not disappoint.

external.name: C.E.B. Cranfield
external.point: Argues that the indicative 'we have peace' is to be preferred despite the subjunctive’s manuscript support: Paul is stating an accomplished fact about the believer’s objective status, not exhorting to an attitude.

1.application: Notice that the Christian life is made to begin not with a demand but with a gift already secured: peace, access, standing, hope. The believer’s assurance rests not on the strength of his grip on God but on the grace in which he has been set to stand — and even his sufferings are enrolled in the service of that hope.

6.exposition: How can Paul be so sure the believer’s hope will not disappoint? Because of the cross. At exactly the right moment, while we were still helpless, Christ died for the ungodly. Paul then presses the sheer unlikelihood of it: one might barely find a man willing to die for a righteous person, though perhaps for a truly good one someone might dare (v.7). But God’s love breaks every human measure — while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us. From that fixed point Paul reasons twice over, from the greater to the lesser: if we were justified by his blood while sinners, much more shall we be saved from wrath through him; if we were reconciled while enemies by his death, much more shall we be saved by his risen life. The section crowns itself in joy in God himself, through the Christ by whom we have received reconciliation.
6.original_language: Two words fix our former condition: asthenēs (“without strength,” helpless, unable to save ourselves) and asebēs (“ungodly,” impious). Against them stands sunistēsin (v.8), God “commends” — that is, proves, exhibits, sets beyond doubt — his own love. The argument of vv.9–10 runs on the refrain pollō mallon (“much more”), the light-to-heavy logic that reasons from the harder deed already done to the easier deed now certain. And v.11 names the prize with katallagē (“reconciliation”) — the restoration of a broken relation, the enmity removed and friendship established; the same root by which Paul elsewhere pleads, “be reconciled to God.”
6.historical_context: The fine distinction in v.7 — scarcely dying for a “righteous” man, perhaps daring it for a “good” one — reflects a real moral calculus in the ancient honour culture. To die for a benefactor who had done great good for you or your city was intelligible within the patron-client world of reciprocity; to die for a stranger or an enemy was not. Paul’s point is that Christ’s death fits neither recognised category: it was for the ungodly, who had rendered God nothing. The doubled pollō mallon is the rabbinic argument form qal wahomer (“light and heavy”): if the lesser holds, how much more the greater. The “wrath to come” (v.9) was a settled feature of Jewish apocalyptic expectation, and “reconciliation” language belonged to both diplomacy and the restoring of estranged parties.
6.christ: Christ died “in due time” (kata kairon, v.6) — not by accident but at the appointed moment of God’s calendar, the fullness of time when God sent forth his Son. Two verbs then divide the work of salvation between the dying and the living Christ: his death justifies, his life saves. The resurrection is therefore as necessary as the cross; the One who died for us now lives for us, and his unbroken intercession is the means by which the reconciliation once accomplished is carried safely through to the end. The love commended at Calvary is not a mood in God but his settled character, publicly displayed toward his enemies while they were still at their worst.

verses.note: “For when we were yet without strength, in due time Christ died for the ungodly.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson gloss asthenēs as helpless — morally powerless to save ourselves — and “in due time” as the divinely fixed hour of God’s plan. puritan Henry and reformed Calvin press the wonder: Christ died not for the righteous but for the impious, for those who had no claim, no strength, and no merit. This is the supreme proof of a love that begins on God’s side alone.

“For scarcely for a righteous man will one die; yet peradventure for a good man some would even dare to die.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the calibrated contrast: among men, hardly anyone would die even for a just man, though for a benevolent, loveable one someone might possibly venture it — a higher love, yet still spent only on the deserving. reformed Calvin sees the whole comparison built to magnify what comes next: even the rarest human self-sacrifice reaches only to the good, but Christ died for sinners.

“But God commendeth his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson unpack sunistēsin — God proves, exhibits, demonstrates his love beyond all doubt. Unlike human love, which waits for worth, God’s love was shown while we were still sinners, his enemies, not yet made lovely. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret call it love beyond compare — the very heart of the gospel and the unshakeable ground of the believer’s assurance.

“Much more then, being now justified by his blood, we shall be saved from wrath through him.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson identify the pollō mallon argument: if God did the harder thing — justifying us by Christ’s blood while we were sinners — much more will he do the easier, delivering us from the wrath to come. The future salvation is thus made as sure as the present justification, both resting on the atoning blood.

“For if, when we were enemies, we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son, much more, being reconciled, we shall be saved by his life.” reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret draw out the two-fold ground: the death reconciled, the life saves; if the costlier work was done for enemies, the lesser is certain for friends. grammatical-historical Robertson points to the risen, living Christ, whose continual intercession secures the salvation his death began.

“And not only so, but we also joy in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, by whom we have now received the atonement.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the climax: the believer not only has peace and assured salvation but joys in God himself — the highest blessing, delight in God. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note that “atonement” here is katallagē, reconciliation — the already-received ground of that joy, the crown of the whole catalogue of blessings.

witnesses.point: Reads the 'much more' of vv.9-10 as an argument from the harder deed already accomplished (justifying sinners) to the easier deed now certain (saving from wrath).
The contrast with human love magnifies God’s: even the rarest human sacrifice is spent only on the good, but Christ died for sinners — love beyond compare.
Christ died not for the righteous but for the impious and helpless, who had no claim, no strength, and no merit — the supreme proof of divine love.
The death reconciled and the risen life saves; 'atonement' in v.11 is reconciliation (katallagē), the already-received ground of the believer’s joy in God.

external.name: Leon Morris
external.point: Stresses that the love displayed at the cross is God’s own love, publicly and decisively demonstrated toward sinners; the reconciliation is Godward, restoring those who were actively hostile.

6.application: The whole weight of Christian confidence is placed here on something outside us: a death that happened at an appointed hour, for enemies, and a life that now pleads for us. When assurance falters, the remedy is not to look harder at the quality of our faith but to look again at the timing of the cross — “while we were yet sinners.”

12.pericope_label: Death Through Adam, Life Through Christ
12.exposition: Paul now goes to the very fountain of the disease in order to measure the cure. Sin entered the world through one man, and death by sin, and so death spread to all — “for that all have sinned,” the most contested clause in the letter. Between Adam and Moses, death reigned even where there was no explicit command to break, proving sin’s universal reach; and Adam, Paul says, is the typos, the pattern, of the Coming One. But the parallel is deliberately unequal: not as the offence, so also is the free gift. Grace does not merely cancel the ruin — it super-abounds. Where judgment ran from one trespass to condemnation, the gift runs from many trespasses to justification. If death reigned through one, much more shall those who receive the gift of righteousness reign in life through One, Jesus Christ. So the two heads stand over their two humanities: by one man’s disobedience many were made sinners; by one man’s obedience many are made righteous. The law entered that the offence might abound, but grace did much more abound, that as sin reigned unto death, grace might reign through righteousness unto eternal life.
12.original_language: The passage is a lattice of key terms. Hamartia (“sin”) eisēlthen (“entered”) the world through one man (v.12) — sin personified as an intruding power. The crux is the little phrase eph hō pantes hēmarton, which may be read “in whom [Adam] all sinned” (so the Latin in quo) or “because all sinned.” Adam is a typos (v.14, “figure, pattern, stamp”) of Christ. The whole comparison is governed by the drumbeat pollō mallon (“much more,” v.15, v.17), which marks not equivalence but asymmetry — the solution overshoots the problem. In v.19 the verb is kathistēmi (“constituted, appointed to a standing”), used of both the “made sinners” and the “made righteous” — a forensic setting-down of status, not an infusion of quality. And in v.20 grace does not merely abound but hypereperisseusen — “super-abounds,” overflowing beyond the flood of sin.
12.historical_context: The “one man” through whom sin and death entered was already a live subject of Jewish reflection. The apocalypse 4 Ezra laments, “O Adam, what have you done? For though it was you who sinned, the fall was not yours alone, but ours also who are your descendants”; 2 Baruch likewise ties Adam’s transgression to the death of all. Paul enters this Second-Temple conversation but carries it somewhere it had never gone — for Judaism had no “second Adam.” The distinction between sin and transgression in vv.13–14 answers a genuine puzzle: if sin is only formally reckoned where there is law, what of the age between Adam and Moses? Paul’s reply — sin was present, though the crossing of a stated boundary required a stated command — is coherent within Jewish legal categories. The logic of corporate solidarity, one representative determining the standing of the many, was already woven into the covenant structure of Israel.
12.christ: Here Christ is unveiled as the last Adam — not yet in that phrase, but in the whole architecture of the passage, which Paul himself makes explicit elsewhere: “as in Adam all die, even so in Christ shall all be made alive,” and “the last Adam was made a quickening spirit.” Adam and Christ are the two federal heads of two humanities, parallel in structure but opposite in effect. What Adam failed to render — the righteousness and obedience proper to the image-bearer of God — Christ supplies in full: his “one righteous act” (dikaiōma, v.18) and his “obedience” (v.19), his whole life of law-keeping crowned by his death, are a single representative deed by which the many are constituted righteous. Where Adam’s trespass opened a reign of death, Christ’s obedience opens a reign of life; the dominion the first man lost, the second Man restores to all who are found in him, so that they shall not merely be spared death but reign in life.

verses.note: “Wherefore, as by one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin… for that all have sinned.” eastern Chrysostom, going like a physician to the fountain of the disease, reads it plainly: Adam having once fallen, even those who had not eaten of the tree became mortal through him. On the disputed eph hō pantes hēmarton the witnesses divide sharply. reformed Calvin — with Augustine and the Latin Vulgate’s in quo (“in whom all sinned”) — reads an inherited corruption: we are all imbued with natural depravity from the womb; and he expressly rejects the Pelagian gloss that sin spread by mere imitation, which would leave Christ only an example and not the cause of righteousness. grammatical-historical Barnes takes eph hō as “because all sinned,” and insists the verse does not here affirm that Adam’s sin is imputed to us, only that all men in fact sin. wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads Adam as the representative of mankind, sin entering with its consequences upon the race he heads. The trespass of Genesis 3:6 thus opens the dark backdrop against which grace will shine.

“For until the law sin was in the world: but sin is not imputed when there is no law.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the period in view — from Adam to Moses, before the written law — in which men sinned and died. Though sin is not formally reckoned where no explicit command has been given, men nevertheless died in that age. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret draw the inference: the reign of death before Sinai proves that sin was already present; the law would later make sin known and aggravate it, but did not first introduce it.

“Nevertheless death reigned from Adam to Moses, even over them that had not sinned after the similitude of Adam’s transgression, who is the figure of him that was to come.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson observe that death held universal sway even over those — including infants — who had broken no positive command as Adam had, which points to their share in his fall. eastern Theodoret and reformed Calvin seize on the last clause: Adam is the typos, the type of Christ — the one whose single act determines the destiny of the many, the very principle the rest of the passage will unfold.

“But not as the offence, so also is the free gift.” reformed Gill identifies the terms: “the offence” is Adam’s first sin, by which he and his posterity fell; “the free gift” is the righteousness of Christ that justifies. Paul’s point is a dissimilarity in Christ’s favour — if by one man’s offence many died, much more has the grace of God abounded to many. The antitype surpasses the type; grace does not merely repair the ruin but overflows it.

“And not as it was by one that sinned, so is the gift.” grammatical-historical Barnes marks this as the second point of difference: the condemnation flowed from a single act of guilt — Adam’s one offence — whereas the free gift has respect to many offences, reaching from a whole multitude of sins to justification. The grace is abundant not only in exceeding the offence but in the sheer breadth of its pardon, covering Adam’s sin and all the believer’s own.

“For if by one man’s offence death reigned by one; much more they which receive abundance of grace… shall reign in life by one, Jesus Christ.” reformed Gill notes it was the single sin of Adam that gave death its reigning power, and grammatical-historical Barnes condenses the same thought. But the “much more” lifts the believer higher than mere rescue: the redeemed are not simply delivered from death’s tyranny but made to reign in life themselves — kings through the one Saviour, a triumph fuller than the ruin it answers.

“Therefore as by the offence of one judgment came upon all men to condemnation; even so by the righteousness of one the free gift came upon all men unto justification of life.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read this as the summing-up of v.12, resumed after the intervening explanation. The two “all” are weighed carefully: most hold the second means all who are in Christ by faith, parallel to all who are in Adam by nature — not that every person without exception is justified, for the gift is received only by union with the second Head. Others press the universal sufficiency of the provision; but the justification of life is actually possessed only by those in Christ.

“For as by one man’s disobedience many were made sinners, so by the obedience of one shall many be made righteous.” The verb is kathistēmi — constituted, set down as, reckoned — used of both clauses. reformed Calvin holds that we are truly made righteous through Christ’s obedience, a righteousness provided in satisfying the Father and received as belonging to him; reformed Hodge frames the governing idea — men regarded and treated not by their own merit but by the merit of another, the representative principle. grammatical-historical Barnes guards the clause carefully: it does not teach that men are condemned for Adam’s act apart from any guilt of their own, for the parallel would then make universal salvation inevitable; men are in fact sinners themselves. Here the great question of imputation is joined — whether Adam’s disobedience is reckoned to the race and Christ’s obedience to the believing, or whether each is only constituted by his own response.

“Moreover the law entered, that the offence might abound. But where sin abounded, grace did much more abound.” reformed Gill takes “the law” chiefly as the moral law given with solemnity at Sinai, coming in between Adam’s sin and Christ’s sacrifice to reveal sin and make it appear exceeding sinful (cf. Romans 7:13). The law does not create sin but exposes and aggravates it. And yet where sin thus abounded, grace hypereperisseusen — super-abounded, overflowing beyond it. This is the believer’s comfort, though Paul will at once guard it against abuse (Romans 6:1): grace abounds to conquer sin, not to indulge it.

“That as sin hath reigned unto death, even so might grace reign through righteousness unto eternal life by Jesus Christ our Lord.” reformed Gill contrasts the two reigns: sin holds a kingly, universal dominion over men in nature, but grace reigns to a contrary end. Grace reigns “through righteousness” — not apart from it, but grounded on the imputed righteousness of Christ — issuing in eternal life, “the gift of God… through Jesus Christ our Lord” (Romans 6:23). So the great argument is crowned with the triumph of reigning grace.

witnesses.point: Paul goes to the fountain of the disease: Adam having fallen, even those who did not eat of the tree became mortal through him.
Reads 'in whom all sinned' — an inherited corruption from Adam — and rejects the Pelagian view that sin spread by mere imitation, which would leave Christ only an example.
Takes 'because all sinned' and denies the verse teaches the imputation of Adam’s sin here; guards v.19 lest a purely representative reading make universal salvation inevitable.
Reads Adam as the representative of mankind, through whom sin and its consequences entered upon the whole race he heads.

external.name: Douglas J. Moo
external.point: Argues that 'all sinned' denotes a real solidarity of the race in and with Adam, not merely the sum of individual acts; and stresses that the repeated 'how much more' marks a deliberate asymmetry — grace does not simply reverse Adam’s trespass but super-abounds beyond it.

12.application: Every person stands under one of two heads. In Adam the story runs downward — one trespass, condemnation, the reign of death over a whole race that never chose him. In Christ the story runs upward and further — one obedience, justification, a reign of grace and life for all who are joined to him by faith. The gospel’s confidence is that the second word is stronger than the first: where sin abounded, grace super-abounded.

_meta.title: Peace with God, and the Second Adam
reflection: Paul piles up the fruits of justification — peace, access, standing, hope — as gifts already secured (vv.1–2). Where are you still living as though your standing with God rose and fell with your performance rather than with Christ’s?

“While we were yet sinners, Christ died for us” (v.8). When your assurance wavers, do you look for firmer evidence in the quality of your faith, or in the timing of the cross — a love shown before you had anything to offer?

Every person stands under one of two heads, Adam or Christ (vv.18–19; 1 Corinthians 15:22). What changes when you reckon that grace does not merely undo Adam’s ruin but super-abounds beyond it, so that in Christ you are made not just safe but a king who reigns in life?
